feedback.text: I am an Italian national but I die in Brazil. I closely monitor the software and games industry, and what I am most inconvenient is that there is hardly any protection for the end user. Businesses do what they want, with no satisfaction. An absurd example was Ubisoft’s The Crew. I have friends who bought the game, but now it simply does not work more. Nor offline. They closed the servers and finished. No reimbursement, no solution, nothing. The user has paid and lost. Another case is Autodesk, which sold perpetual licences from AutoCAD, and then decided to disable access. They forced all over the world to go pro model signature. Many people paid for something that was theoretically always bought and now need to pay monthly buy to continue using. This happened in my work in Brazil, for example. It seems a distant, equivalent to buying a tool, a book, a game and then inventing my place of residence or work to remove it. Therefore, I fully support the Digital Fairness Act. Europe can and should lead by example in the world with laws that protect consumers in the digital environment. Prohibiting such abuses. I bought a product, I have the right to use it.
